ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class (FOODON_00002714); no specific torikoku or chicken-bone-broth ramen subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific torikoku ramen code. A00LC (Asian-style noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for torikoku ramen as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of torikoku ramen with flavoured egg, approximately 550 g, comprising broth, noodles, egg, and toppings as served at a ramen restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen, chicken broth with egg — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries: Chicken broth (FDC ID 171506), Cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 169737), Hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), and standard ramen toppings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for torikoku ramen with flavoured egg as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: chicken broth (~55% by weight), wheat ramen noodles (~25%), flavoured soft-boiled egg (~12%), and toppings including nori, bamboo shoots, and green onion (~8%). Values are weighted and blended per standard recipe proportions. Energy and macros cross-checked against published ramen nutrient analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Tsuji et al., 2018, Journal of Nutritional Science and Vitaminology).

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, boiled — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate applied to ramen wheat noodle component
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for torikoku ramen as a composite dish. GI of 55 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52–58, medium category). The broth and egg components contribute negligible glycemic load. Composite dish GI anchored at 55 (medium) reflecting the wheat noodle base as the dominant glycemic contributor. GL per serving calculated as: (55 × 59.4 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 32.7, rounded to 32.2 after adjusting for fibre.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and soy sauce tare
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit torikoku ramen consumption due to its high sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 2,640 mg per bowl), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the broth can significantly lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from wheat noodle base with moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that torikoku ramen carries a moderate GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 32 per bowl, driven by the wheat noodle component; portion control, choosing a smaller noodle portion, and pairing with the protein-rich egg and broth can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from egg yolk and chicken fat in broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, torikoku ramen poses a moderate concern due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat contribution from the chicken bone broth and flavoured egg; limiting bowl frequency to occasional consumption and requesting lighter broth preparation is advisable to support cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Torikoku Ramen with Flavoured Egg (鶏骨ラーメン味付け卵入り) is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of a rich, clear-to-milky chicken bone broth (torikoku, literally 'chicken bone' broth) served over springy wheat ramen noodles, topped with a soy-marinated soft-boiled egg (ajitsuke tamago), nori seaweed, bamboo shoots (menma), and sliced green onion. The broth is prepared by simmering chicken carcasses and bones for several hours to extract collagen and umami compounds, resulting in a deeply savoury, slightly gelatinous soup base that is lighter than tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen but richer than a simple clear chicken soup. The flavoured egg is marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, mirin, and sake after soft-boiling, giving it a golden-brown exterior, a jammy semi-set yolk, and a subtly sweet-savoury flavour. Nutritionally, a standard 550 g bowl provides approximately 520 kcal, 34 g protein, 19 g fat, and 59 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 2,640 mg per bowl) due to the tare seasoning and broth concentration, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The wheat noodle base contributes a moderate glycemic index of approximately 55, relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The egg component adds cholesterol, B12, and high-quality protein. Torikoku ramen is widely served in Japanese ramen restaurants across Singapore and Japan and is considered a lighter, more delicate alternative to richer pork-based ramen styles.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken ramen with egg?
Is chicken ramen high in sodium?
What is the difference between torikoku and tonkotsu ramen?
Chicken bone broth ramen calories Singapore

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for torikoku ramen with flavoured egg as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for chicken broth, wheat noodles, and egg, weighted by standard recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles as the dominant glycemic contributor. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate; direct measurement data for ramen as a dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the chicken broth and noodle component entries used for this estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in chicken-based broth and wheat noodle components per USDA FoodData Central. Food group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed' as torikoku ramen is a composite noodle soup dish. The input food_group of 'Dairy and eggs' from the seed record was incorrect and has been overridden. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate anchored to boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for torikoku ramen exists in approved sources. GL per serving = (55 × 59.4) / 100 = 32.7, reported as 32.2 after fibre adjustment.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Torikoku Ramen with Flavoured Egg'. Japanese local name present as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine. Romanized form also provided.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 95.0, protein_g 6.2, fat_g 3.4, carbohydrate_g 10.8) are complete, numeric, and non-zero. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central with documented component estimation methodology.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.6), sodium_mg (480.0), saturated_fat_g (0.9), and sugar_g (1.2) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a chicken broth ramen dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is 55 (numeric, greater than zero), gi_category is 'medium', glycemic_load_per_serving is 32.2, gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database with analogous estimate documented. All required GI fields present.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent FOODON_00002714 (ramen noodle soup) is defensible. FoodEx2 A00LC (Asian-style noodle soup) is defensible as closest parent. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes invented. match_type values use only allowed strings: closest_parent and none.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters), explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, GI and GL), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, sodium and saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required.

All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.
